html:
Woman was created from a rib — "the top is crooked" Women Cosmology Moderate Nasa'i cross-ref #9168
"Treat women kindly — she is created from a rib, its top is the most crooked."

What the hadith says

Nasa'i preserves the Genesis-derived origin story with the specific addition that women's nature is like the top of a rib — inherently crooked.

Why this is a problem

The "crooked rib" metaphor characterises women's fundamental nature as structurally bent, and the counsel of kindness is explicitly predicated on that crookedness — "treat her gently because she's crooked" is the logic. A Hebrew Bible folk myth about human origin is imported as prophetic teaching and used to ground a claim about female character. The instruction is framed as chivalric advice while encoding a belief that women's nature is defective.

Why it fails

An accommodation-with-kindness reading does not neutralise the "crooked" characterisation. Describing women's nature as inherently bent — and grounding that in a folk-origin myth about a physical deformity — is a claim about female character regardless of whether kindness accompanies it. Chivalry premised on deficiency is patronising, not respectful, and the deficiency claim is the hadith's explicit content.

Prophet exiled the mukhannathun from Medina LGBTQ / Gender Prophetic Character Moderate Nasa'i #5102
"The Prophet cursed men who imitate women and women who imitate men — and he exiled the effeminate ones."

What the hadith says

Gender-nonconforming men were cursed and expelled from Medina — a prophetic precedent preserved with explicit reference to their manner of gender expression.

Why this is a problem

The curse is for mannerisms, not actions — for walking, speaking, or presenting in a way associated with the other sex. Exile followed from presentation alone, establishing a prophetic precedent for excluding people based on gender expression. Contemporary state-level enforcement against gender-nonconforming individuals in multiple Muslim-majority jurisdictions cites this and parallel hadiths as prophetic warrant. A religion that curses people for how they walk has aimed its disapproval at the shape of personality itself.

Why it fails

The deliberate-performance distinction does not survive the hadith's scope: the exile applied to multiple named individuals based on presentation, and the curse applies broadly to anyone who "imitates" the other sex — a behavioural standard with no innate-disposition exception. Classical jurisprudence built an enduring exclusionary category that extended well beyond privacy-violation cases. A tradition that curses and exiles based on mannerisms has aimed at the expression itself, and the "innate vs. deliberate" distinction is a modern apologetic overlay the text does not support.

Prophet saw hell — most of its people were women Women Hell Moderate Nasa'i #1493
"I was shown hellfire, and I saw that most of its inhabitants are women."

What the hadith says

Muhammad's vision of hell recorded a female majority among the damned, attributed to ingratitude toward husbands and excessive cursing.

Why this is a problem

A prophetic vision of hell with a female majority is a theological statement about women as a category, preserved across multiple canonical collections with explicit attribution to mundane social behaviours. The cited reasons — ingratitude and cursing — are gendered behavioural stereotypes whose attribution to women rather than men is itself a cultural judgment. A religion whose prophet describes a disproportionately female hell has made a structural statement about half its adherents that the Quran's spiritual-equality verses do not neutralise.

Why it fails

If the observation is local to the Prophet's community, it should not function as eternal theology — but it has, across fourteen centuries of Islamic preaching. If the demographic applies across all generations, the claim is about women as a category. Q 33:35's spiritual equality does not resolve a prophetic vision that specifically maps hellfire demography to female gender. The tradition cannot simultaneously affirm female spiritual equality and preserve a prophetic hell-census that contradicts it.

Prophet rotated nights equally — until revelation let him skip Prophetic Character Women Moderate Nasa'i #3952
"The Prophet used to allocate one night to each wife; when Q 33:51 was revealed, he could defer whomever he willed."

What the hadith says

A revelation arrived relieving Muhammad of the equal-rotation obligation he had until that point maintained among his wives.

Why this is a problem

The timing is the argument: revelation relaxed the obligation at precisely the moment it had become inconvenient. Aisha's preserved sarcasm — "your Lord hastens to fulfill your wishes" — is the internal critique the tradition itself preserved. Marital equal-treatment was established as the norm, then abrogated by a verse, for one man. A revelation whose pattern tracks the Prophet's personal circumstances is a revelation whose source requires examination.

Why it fails

If the rotation created conflict, the obligation to rotate was itself a divine contribution to household conflict. The sequence — divine obligation creates problem; divine verse removes obligation — describes a divine law that had to be revised to fix the problems it caused. Aisha identified this pattern in real time, and the tradition preserved her identification. The apologetic improvement-framing requires accepting that the first ruling was flawed enough to need fixing.

Aisha's toy horses with wings — picture ban exemption Child Marriage Prophetic Character Moderate Nasa'i #3381
"Aisha played with a horse that had two wings made of cloth; the Prophet laughed."

What the hadith says

Aisha's child play with figurines — elsewhere banned under the picture-making prohibition — is preserved approvingly, with the Prophet laughing at her winged-horse toy.

Why this is a problem

The hadith creates two simultaneous problems. First, the picture-making prohibition (that angels will not enter homes containing images) is exempted for Aisha's toys on no stated principle — special treatment for a child in the Prophet's household. Second, Aisha's developmental age at the time of cohabitation is unambiguously present in the narrative: a wife who still keeps toy horses has an age profile that no amount of apologetic elaboration about pre-modern age conventions can resolve. The tradition preserved the detail candidly, which is why it cannot be rescued.

Why it fails

The image-exemption for children's toys is itself a post-hoc juristic construction built on this hadith — the exception exists because of the case, not independent of it. The affection-and-normalcy framing cannot absorb the underlying incongruity: a wife old enough for consummation still playing with winged-horse figurines. The tradition's own candour is the apologetic's undoing — both facts are preserved simultaneously, and they cannot be reconciled by warmth.

Do not greet Jews and Christians first — force them to the narrow part of the road Disbelievers Antisemitism Moderate Nasa'i tradition paralleling Muslim #2167
Nasa'i preserves the same social-humiliation hadith: Muslims must push non-Muslims to the narrow side of streets and not initiate greetings.

What the hadith says

A physical and verbal humiliation ritual is prescribed for Muslim-non-Muslim street encounters — no first greeting, narrow-side assignment.

Why this is a problem

Ritualised social subjugation is a systematic daily reminder of rank, not an incidental practice. The narrow-street rule and greeting prohibition together constitute an interpersonal discrimination code that structures every casual encounter between Muslims and non-Muslims as a status performance. This is not tolerance under a dhimma framework — it is a daily ceremony of subordination. The rule appears across multiple canonical collections, confirming it was a consistent institutional position rather than an outlier.

Why it fails

Daily street-side humiliation as "symbolic marker of political hierarchy" is a self-description that describes the experience from the top. From the non-Muslim's position, being physically displaced to the narrow side of the road and refused a first greeting by their neighbours is systematic public degradation, regardless of what accompanying legal protections they received. Modern abandonment is a departure from classical law, not a rehabilitation of it.

The Khawarij — "dogs of Hellfire" Apostasy & Blasphemy Hell Moderate Nasa'i tradition paralleling Muslim #1066
"They are the dogs of Hellfire."

What the hadith says

A prophetic anathema against the Kharijite sectarian movement — dehumanising language assigned to theological dissenters by the Prophet himself.

Why this is a problem

Theological pre-damnation of dissenters using subhuman language — "dogs" — sets a template for handling theological opposition that has proven durable: every subsequent dissenting movement in Islamic history has faced the same label applied by the dominant tradition. Mutazilites, Ismailis, Ahmadis, and modern reform movements have all been compared to the Khawarij using this hadith. A prophetic precedent of theological dehumanisation is what makes mutual takfir structurally available — the tool outlasted the original target by fourteen centuries.

Why it fails

The restriction to historical Kharijites is aspirational — the same hadith has been applied to non-violent reformers throughout Islamic history. A prophetic pre-damnation phrased as broadly as "dogs of hellfire" does not come with enforceable scope-limits. The welcome modern application against ISIS does not change the structural problem: a dehumanising prophetic formula aimed at theological opponents has been used, and will continue to be used, as an orthodoxy weapon against any group sufficiently disfavoured by the tradition's mainstream.

The Prophet's exclusive intercession on Judgment Day Prophetic Privileges Contradictions Moderate Nasa'i tradition paralleling Muslim #193
"Every Prophet has a supplication that he used on earth — I have saved mine for intercession for my Ummah on the Day of Resurrection."

What the hadith says

Muhammad reserved his guaranteed prophetic supplication specifically for Judgment Day intercession — a privilege exclusive to him and available only for Muslims.

Why this is a problem

Q 2:48 and Q 2:123 both deny that intercession will avail on Judgment Day. The hadith reinstates what the Quran denied and concentrates it in the Prophet alone, making Muhammad a unique mediator whose intercession determines who among the Muslim community is saved from major sins. A religion that marketed itself as abolishing priestly mediation has rebuilt the institution as a single exclusive prophet-mediator — functionally indistinguishable from what it claimed to supersede.

Why it fails

Q 2:48 says "no intercession will be accepted" — not "no intercession without permission." The permission-exception is a qualification supplied by the apologetic tradition to reconcile a contradiction, not a reading supported by the verse's text. The structure is circular: the verse denies intercession; the hadith creates a permitted exception; classical theology harmonises them by assuming the exception was always implied. The contradiction is resolved by assuming the Quran meant something narrower than it said.

Jews "hid" the stoning verse — Muhammad exposed it Antisemitism Scripture Integrity Moderate Nasa'i #5407
"A rabbi was asked about the punishment of adultery in the Torah; he put his hand over the verse of stoning. The Prophet had him lift his hand."

What the hadith says

A theatrical scene: a rabbi physically hiding a Torah stoning-verse with his palm, exposed by Muhammad.

Why this is a problem

The Torah's stoning texts (Deuteronomy 22) are not secret — they are in every Torah scroll and have been publicly available throughout Jewish history. The scene is staged polemic, not historical encounter: a rabbi who physically covers a page to hide it from an interlocutor asking about that exact subject is a cartoon villain, not a plausible historical figure. The accusation of tahrif (scriptural tampering) rests on theatrical vignettes like this, not on textual evidence of actual alteration.

Why it fails

The embarrassment-reading requires the rabbi to have covered a non-secret text theatrically to avoid acknowledging it — which is a plausible human behaviour but does not support the tahrif accusation. The hadith was used to establish that Jews hid and tampered with their scripture, a charge that has driven anti-Jewish polemic in Islamic discourse for centuries. The incident's theatrical staging is the signature of a polemical vignette, not a historical record.

Jesus returns — breaks crosses, kills pigs, abolishes jizya Jesus / Christology Eschatology Moderate Nasa'i tradition paralleling Muslim #155
"Jesus will descend and break the cross, kill the swine, and abolish the jizya — because nothing will remain except Islam."

What the hadith says

Jesus's second coming is portrayed as a programme of anti-Christian actions — destroying the symbols of his own tradition and eliminating the non-Muslim exemption that allowed Christians to coexist under Islamic rule.

Why this is a problem

"Abolishing jizya" means conversion or death: the dhimma option — which permitted non-Muslims to live as protected minorities — ends, leaving only the convert-or-fight binary. The Christian messiah returns to destroy Christianity's central symbol (the cross), criminalise one of its dietary norms (swine), and remove the legal status that allowed Christians to exist as Christians under Islamic rule. This is not rectification of a distorted religion — it is eschatological supersessionism with enforceable consequences.

Why it fails

"Voluntary conversion" following the removal of all legal alternatives is not voluntary in any meaningful sense. A prophecy in which Jesus destroys his followers' symbols, eliminates their dietary freedom, and removes the legal framework that allows them to remain Christian has absorbed Christianity only to annul it. The "rectification" framing is Islamic self-description; the structural outcome is forced conversion or death — which is the opposite of religious freedom regardless of the theological justification offered.

Allah descends nightly to the lowest heaven Allah's Character Cosmology Moderate Nasa'i #1619
"Our Lord descends to the lowest heaven each night, when the last third of the night remains."

What the hadith says

Allah physically descends nightly to the lowest heaven — a directional movement contradicting the attribute of omnipresence.

Why this is a problem

An omnipresent being cannot be in one place more than another, so descent is incoherent. More concretely, the "last third of the night" is always occurring somewhere on a rotating earth — if the descent tracks the night's last third globally, Allah descends continuously and permanently; if locally, the descent is not nightly but constant in some location. Both readings expose a flat-earth cosmology where night and day have fixed global boundaries, not a rotating-sphere cosmology.

Why it fails

If the descent is metaphorical, the hadith communicates nothing specific about divine behaviour — only that Allah is accessible, which is already Quranic teaching. The metaphor-reading defuses the cosmological problem at the cost of making the hadith theologically redundant. The rotating-earth problem remains: a God who descends to the last-third-of-night zone is a God imagined in a flat-earth framework.

"The rulers must be from Quraysh" Governance Contradictions Moderate Nasa'i #5913
"This matter will remain in the hands of the Quraysh as long as they rule justly."

What the hadith says

Legitimate Muslim rulership is restricted to a single tribe — Quraysh — a hereditary qualification for political authority.

Why this is a problem

Most Muslim rulers for the past millennium were not Qurayshi — the Abbasids ended in 1258, and subsequent Mamluk, Ottoman, Safavid, Mughal, and all modern Muslim heads of state fall outside the requirement. By the hadith's own logic, Islamic governance has been technically illegitimate for most of its history. A religion that restricts leadership to one bloodline has replaced revolutionary equality with tribal theocracy, and then had to explain fourteen centuries of non-Qurayshi rule.

Why it fails

The multiple apologetic exits are evidence of a ruling the tradition cannot sustain in its plain form. A qualification that disqualifies most of Islamic governance history is either not a binding rule (in which case it should not be called prophetic guidance) or is a binding rule that has been systematically violated (in which case it condemns most Islamic governance as illegitimate). Neither outcome is comfortable, and the tradition has negotiated between them without resolution.
